frameworks.name: nonlinear dynamical systems
frameworks.one_line: Neural circuits modeled as nonlinear dynamical systems to be steered

frameworks.name: control theory
frameworks.one_line: Control-theoretic design of inputs that drive activity to a target

frameworks.name: recurrent network models
frameworks.one_line: Recurrent network simulations serve as the testbed for the method

frameworks.name: data-driven methods
frameworks.one_line: Spontaneous-activity statistics used to identify high-leverage perturbation sites

core_question: Can spatiotemporal patterns of multi-site stimulation be designed to steer neural activity and behavior toward a desired target?
key_assumptions: Effective perturbation sites can be inferred from short observations of spontaneous activity
Network dynamics are sufficiently captured by recurrent dynamical models
Stimulation efficacy and selectivity can be jointly optimized
manipulations: Selection of perturbation sites (hubs) from spontaneous activity
Design of multi-site spatiotemporal stimulation patterns
Combination of control-theoretic and data-driven design steps
key_findings: A two-step pipeline identifies hub sites and designs multi-site stimulation patterns
Hubs can be identified from short spontaneous-activity observations alone
Feasibility demonstrated with multi-site stimulation in recurrent network models
interpretations: Model-based design can replace laborious trial-and-error for complex multi-site perturbations
Combining dynamical systems, control, and data-driven tools enables precise causal manipulation
limitations_noted: Demonstration is in recurrent network models rather than in vivo
